posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto introduces the concept of the 'new normal' - the phenomenon where your perception of what's normal shifts based on the people you surround yourself with, fundamentally changing your ambitions and likelihood of success. He traces four pivotal moments in his life: accidentally joining an online BBS community of game hackers at age 12, receiving a job opportunity through his open source work, deliberately choosing to move to San Francisco, and gaining the confidence to start HashiCorp. The essay argues that these shifts in normality can happen through serendipity, opportunity, deliberate choice, or confidence-building. He emphasizes that actively seeking a new normal can steer your life in unexpected directions.
key_points: Your perception of 'normal' is shaped by the people you surround yourself with, which directly impacts your ambitions and achievements
The progression from serendipity to opportunity to deliberate choice represents increasing agency over your own trajectory
Immersing yourself in communities of people doing what you aspire to do makes those aspirations feel achievable
The decision to start HashiCorp wasn't part of any plan - it emerged from a shifted sense of what was possible after moving to San Francisco
Choosing who you surround yourself with is one of the most powerful ways to change your life's direction
money_quotes: The 'new normal' is what I've named a phenomenon I've seen multiple times in my life. It's an event where your perception of normality is changed by the people surrounding you.
Actively seeking a new normal can steer you in a direction you never thought you'd take.
None of my real life friends understood what I was doing, and my parents were concerned that I was acting abnormally.
Instead of waiting for things to happen, I chose to take the initiative and move to a new city where I had no friends in order to surround myself with what I wanted to become.
It was normal to relentlessly follow your passion. It was normal to believe that you could chase big dreams at unsurmountable odds and succeed. It was normal to start a company, at any age.

I was told many times before that I should start my own business working on Vagrant. But it wasn't until my perception of normality changed from my choice to move to San Francisco that I was able to gain the confidence to really take the leap.
posts.key_insight: The people you surround yourself with fundamentally reshape what you believe is possible, and deliberately choosing those people is one of the most powerful decisions you can make.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto explains how the APPLE customer service framework he learned as an Apple Store employee became foundational to his entrepreneurial success. The acronym guides interactions through warm approach, polite probing, presenting solutions, listening, and fond farewells. He demonstrates the principle through a real support email exchange where instead of dismissing a canceling customer, he engaged genuinely, fixed the underlying product issue, and invited them back. This philosophy helped him build three successful projects including the one that funded HashiCorp and Vagrant.
key_points: The APPLE framework (Approach, Probe, Present, Listen, End) from Apple retail training applies directly to running products and businesses
Being respectful and caring toward users is the most important factor in building successful projects, even when there's no immediate return
The best solution isn't just one that solves the customer's problem—it's the one that solves it best, often requiring extra effort
A cancellation request is an opportunity to understand, improve, and potentially win back a customer through genuine engagement
Success came from three projects: a passive income business that funded HashiCorp, Vagrant, and IRCRelay which became profitable in 6 weeks
money_quotes: When doing anything, there is nothing more important than being respectful to your customer or user.
All that matters is being a good, caring person.
A solution isn't just something that solves the customer's problem, it is the solution that solves the customer's problem best.
The solution took extra time and effort, and maybe Brian won't even sign up again. But it doesn't matter. It was the right thing to do.
I genuinely do care about making the experience the best it can be for every customer.
A little bit of APPLE goes a long way.
posts.key_insight: Treating every customer interaction with genuine care and solving problems at their root—not just addressing symptoms—is what separates successful products from failed ones.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto explains why Vagrant 1.1+ abandoned RubyGems in favor of pre-made installers and packages. The decision was driven by three factors: most Vagrant users aren't Ruby developers and found RubyGems installation burdensome, dependency mismatches caused bugs that were perceived as Vagrant instability, and bundling dependencies gave him flexibility to swap in better libraries without user friction. By controlling the entire environment, he could replace slow pure-Ruby implementations with high-performance C libraries like libarchive and cURL. The change ultimately made Vagrant more accessible, stable, and performant for its growing user base.
key_points: Most Vagrant users are not Ruby developers, and RubyGems was a significant barrier to entry that prevented adoption
Dependency mismatches and misconfigured environments caused crashes that users blamed on Vagrant's instability
Bundled installers allow shipping optimized C libraries (libarchive, cURL) without requiring users to install additional dependencies
Controlling the runtime environment enables making significant dependency changes transparently, improving the user experience without friction
Distribution choices should evolve as a project's user base grows beyond its original community
money_quotes: Prior to making installers, the top complaint I would receive about Vagrant was about RubyGems.

For anyone outside the Ruby community, I learned that the burden of having to learn RubyGems, install Ruby, and so on was a high enough barrier to entry that many people didn't even try to install Vagrant.
I don't care whether this is perceived as my fault or the user's fault.

It is a huge problem when Vagrant crashes due to a broken environment, and then people perceive this as an instability in Vagrant itself, when 9 times out of 10, it was due to a misconfigured environment.
With installers, I am able to ship Vagrant with all the dependencies it needs, and promise the user that Vagrant will work with those dependencies.
All the user notices is that the Vagrant experience just got a lot better. Things got faster, things don't crash, and Vagrant supports more features.
posts.key_insight: Developer tools should optimize for the actual user base rather than the original community, and controlling the full dependency stack enables both reliability and the freedom to make improvements users benefit from without noticing.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto reflects on his career trajectory and realizes that automation has been his driving passion from the very beginning. Starting with cheating at Neopets as a kid, he progressed through automating ad clicks for money, college course registration, and eventually created Vagrant and founded HashiCorp. Each project followed the same pattern: identifying tedious manual processes and building software to eliminate them. He concludes that automation isn't just what he does—it defines who he is.
key_points: His programming journey began with a desire to automate Neopets tasks, eventually scaling to millions of virtual currency per hour through market arbitrage
Early experiments in automating real-money schemes proved lucrative but ended with legal notices to his parents
College registration automation demonstrated how software checking thousands of times per hour will always beat humans manually refreshing browsers
At Kiip, his automation obsession reached the point where he could rebuild a 50-machine production cluster from scratch in under 30 minutes
The pattern across all his work is consistent: if he had to do anything twice, he would write a program to do it instead
money_quotes: I started programming because I wanted to cheat Neopets.
I found more things to automate, realizing that computers can do tasks much more efficiently than humans, thus unlocking opportunities that didn't exist before.
At this point, I wasn't particularly interested in Neopets anymore. The automation had taken hold within me, and I was just seeing how far I could push it.
While my friends were spending hours every day refreshing course schedules hoping to get into a full class, I was just waiting for a text message.
A human refreshing a browser can't beat my software that was checking thousands of times per hour. Automation wins again.
My obsession with automation reached a point where if I had to do anything twice, I would write a program to do it for me.
Automation defines who I am, and always has.
posts.key_insight: The most successful technologists often discover their defining obsession early—for Hashimoto, every project from Neopets bots to HashiCorp followed the same core drive: eliminating manual work through software.

posts.summary: This post introduces the 'Tao of Vagrant' - a philosophy for using Vagrant to create consistent, reproducible development environments. Developers can check out any repository, run 'vagrant up', and have a fully functional environment without manual setup. The same automation scripts used in development are also used in production, ensuring parity between environments. The workflow is transferable across projects and companies, eliminating 'works on my machine' bugs.
key_points: Vagrant enables any developer to run 'vagrant up' and get a working development environment with zero manual configuration
The same provisioning scripts used for development environments are used in production, ensuring consistency
Development environments can be destroyed and recreated from scratch at any time, providing a clean slate
The Vagrant workflow is universal - once learned, it transfers to any project or company following these principles
Vagrant should be invisible to developers - they work in their preferred editors and tools while Vagrant handles environment setup
money_quotes: In a world with Vagrant, developers can check out any repository from version control, run vagrant up, and have a fully running development environment without any human interaction.
The existence of Vagrant is transparent and unimportant in the mind of the developer.
With every vagrant up, developers are getting fully provisioned development environments using the same scripts that are used to setup production.

If something goes wrong, or they just want to start over from a clean slate, developers and operations engineers can run vagrant destroy, which removes all traces of that development environment from their machine.
Never again can developers forget to shut down a stray server process and waste precious compute resources.
Whether working on project A, project B, or even at company A or company B, as long as they follow the Tao of Vagrant, the workflow is exactly the same.
As a result, productivity abounds and 'works on my machine' bugs disappear.
posts.key_insight: The ideal developer tool should be invisible - it does its job perfectly while the developer remains focused on their actual work, not the tooling.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces Packer, an open source tool for creating machine images across multiple platforms from a single template. Packer addresses the gap between powerful machine image concepts and the tooling needed for modern cloud workflows. The tool can generate AMIs, VirtualBox images, and Vagrant boxes in parallel, enabling use cases like continuous delivery pipelines and synchronized production/development environments. While version 0.1 has limitations, Hashimoto draws parallels to Vagrant's early days, expressing confidence the project will grow with community involvement.
key_points: Packer uses a single portable template format to generate machine images for multiple platforms simultaneously
Existing tooling for machine images hasn't kept pace with modern cloud infrastructure's agile workflow demands
Key use cases include automated AMI creation for continuous delivery and creating nearly identical production/development environments
The project follows the same community-driven growth model as Vagrant, which started with significant limitations that were removed over time
money_quotes: Machine images are a powerful concept, but the existing tooling around them hasn't caught up to the agile workflow that modern cloud infrastructures enable.
Packer uses a single portable input format -- a template -- to generate the images for multiple platforms in parallel.
Packer is a general purpose image building tool. The opportunities to build things around it are endless.
When I released version 0.1 of Vagrant, it only worked on Mac, only worked with VirtualBox, only ran Ubuntu machines, and only worked with serverless Chef.
As the Vagrant community grew so did its capabilities, and now all those limitations have been removed.
posts.key_insight: A single portable template that generates identical machine images across multiple platforms closes the gap between infrastructure-as-code ideals and practical DevOps workflows.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto presents comprehensive benchmarks comparing filesystem performance across different virtual machine configurations, focusing on VirtualBox, VMware, and NFS shared folders. The analysis reveals that VMware shared folders dramatically outperform VirtualBox for reads (sometimes by 70x), while NFS provides excellent read performance but terrible write performance due to network overhead. Interestingly, virtual machine filesystems often outperform the host machine's native filesystem because hypervisors buffer operations and lie about synced writes. The practical takeaway is to use NFS when possible for read-heavy workloads, move database files out of shared folders, and recognize that VMware's read performance advantage matters more than VirtualBox's marginal write advantage for typical development work.
key_points: VMware shared folder read performance crushes VirtualBox (7 GB/s vs never above 100 MB/s for large random reads), suggesting VirtualBox has a fundamental bottleneck in their implementation
NFS provides incredible read performance due to read-ahead caching but suffers terrible write performance from network overhead requiring full round-trip acknowledgment
Virtual machine filesystems often outperform the host's native filesystem because hypervisors buffer operations and don't truly sync writes to disk
For development environments, read performance matters far more than write performance since compiling, testing, and loading pages are read-heavy operations
Moving database files out of shared filesystems and onto native VM storage can provide huge performance benefits
money_quotes: VirtualBox throughput never peaks above 100 MB/s. VMware, on the other hand, is peaking at 7 GB/s.

Because the deviation of the VirtualBox throughput is so small across various test cases, I theorize that there is a single hot path of code in the VirtualBox shared folder system limiting this. They're clearly doing something wrong.
fsync() in a virtual machine does not mean that the data was written on the host, only that it is committed within the hypervisor.
Hypervisor read/write performance is fantastic (because they cheat).
If you have the option to use NFS, use it. Again, the read performance is far more valuable than the write performance.
For years, the primary bottleneck for virtual machine based development environments with Vagrant has been filesystem performance.
posts.key_insight: VMware dramatically outperforms VirtualBox for shared folder reads, NFS excels at reads but fails at writes, and hypervisors gain performance by lying about write synchronization—making the choice of filesystem mechanism critical for development environment performance.

posts.summary: This post explains how the Zig programming language's tokenizer works as the first step in the compiler pipeline. The tokenizer converts source code bytes into a stream of tokens without performing any heap allocations, operating on slices rather than streams. Each token consists of a tag (one of ~120 types) and a location (start/end indices into the source). The implementation uses a state machine that examines one character at a time, switching between states until it unambiguously determines each token. Importantly, tokenizers don't handle semantics—they'll happily tokenize nonsensical code, leaving validation to the parser.
key_points: Zig's tokenizer performs zero heap allocations, which is immediately apparent from its API since it doesn't take an Allocator parameter
Tokens store only a tag enum and start/end indices rather than copying strings—a common efficiency trick
The tokenizer uses a state machine that looks at one character at a time without peeking forward or looking backward
Tokenizers are deliberately not concerned with semantics; invalid syntax like 'if while comptime' produces valid tokens
The implementation uses nested while loops and switch statements on both state and current character
money_quotes: Tokenizers usually don't handle semantics or whether a set of tokens are meaningful.
In systems programming, it's always useful to understand the memory usage and allocation properties of an API.
With Zig, this is immediately obvious since the Tokenizer doesn't take an Allocator as a parameter for anything.
Storing only the start and end is a common efficiency trick.
The tokenizer only looks at the state and the current character; it does not look backward, and it does not peek forward.
A tokenizer is not concerned with semantics, so non-sensical inputs such as 'if while comptime x = 7 { else }' produce a valid stream of tokens.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed tokenizer maintains strict separation of concerns—it converts bytes to tokens without allocations or semantic validation, leaving meaning-making entirely to the parser.

posts.summary: This post provides a deep technical dive into how the Zig compiler's parser transforms tokens into an abstract syntax tree. The parser heavily uses MultiArrayList, a data structure that stores each struct field in a separate contiguous array, reducing memory waste from alignment and improving cache locality. AST nodes use a compact representation where data can reference the token stream, other nodes, or an extra_data array, with the meaning of each field determined by the node's tag. The parser uses a recursive descent approach, eating tokens and building up nodes while attempting to continue parsing through errors. This data-oriented design pattern is reused throughout the compiler pipeline.
key_points: MultiArrayList splits struct fields into separate contiguous arrays, saving 37.5% memory in the example case by eliminating alignment padding
AST node data uses heavy indirection: lhs/rhs fields may point to other nodes OR extra_data, with the interpretation dependent on the node's tag
The parser attempts to continue through errors rather than halting, accumulating errors for later reporting
Understanding this data layout pattern is critical because it's reused throughout the entire Zig compiler pipeline
The parser uses recursive descent with 'eat' and 'expect' token functions to validate syntax while building the tree
money_quotes: This is a particularly critical detail since the AST uses a pattern that is reused throughout the compilation pipeline for other intermediary forms.
Compiling a real world program usually generates tens of thousands of 'nodes' so this results in huge memory savings and improvements in cache locality.

Unlike the tokenizer, the parser cares about whether the token order makes sense. The tokenizer just blindly creates tokens, so invalid syntax such as 'fn pub var foo i32 } {' produces a valid token stream.
If you're interested in knowing how the remainder of the Zig compiler works, it is extremely important that you deeply understand this information storage pattern.
Important point: lhs/rhs may point to either nodes or extra data.
Most well-behaved parsers attempt to continue parsing in various error scenarios and Zig's parser is no different.
posts.key_insight: Zig's parser achieves significant performance gains through data-oriented design, using MultiArrayList and indirect references that sacrifice code simplicity for memory efficiency and cache locality—a pattern that pervades the entire compiler.

posts.summary: This technical deep-dive explains how the Zig compiler converts its Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) into Zig Intermediate Representation (ZIR) through a stage called AstGen. ZIR is an untyped intermediate format that breaks program logic into fine-grained instructions, preparing code for comptime evaluation and further semantic analysis. The post meticulously covers ZIR instruction anatomy, including how references work, extra data encoding, and the role of scopes and string interning. It concludes by walking through progressively complex examples—from integer literals to addition to variable assignment—demonstrating how the recursive generation process builds up ZIR from simpler components.
key_points: ZIR is untyped because Zig's comptime evaluation means types may not be known until later stages—types are first-class values that can be computed dynamically
ZIR instructions use a compact encoding scheme with tagged refs for common primitives and instruction indices for everything else, enabling efficient memory usage
AstGen introduces the first significant layer of semantic validation, checking that identifiers are defined and don't shadow outer scopes
Scopes are polymorphic structures that enable identifier resolution by traversing parent scopes, with seven different scope types serving different purposes
The recursive generation process builds complex constructs from simpler ones—understanding integer literals helps understand addition, which helps understand assignment
money_quotes: Whereas the AST is a tree, the IR usually begins creating a sequence of instructions for various blocks of a program.
A language-defining feature of Zig is that it uses types as first-class values and comptime-evaluation as a mechanism for generic typing.
ZIR is the prepared intermediary form for comptime evaluation and further semantic analysis.
After AstGen, the AST and source can be freed. This enables parsing extremely large Zig programs and storing only what we need in memory.
AstGen introduces our first significant layer of semantic validation.
I find it easiest to start with the simplest components of a language and then build up from there, since IR generation is a deeply recursive process.
If I started with something like a function declaration, I'd get bogged down in so many details that it'd be overwhelming to learn.
posts.key_insight: Understanding compiler internals becomes manageable when you start with the simplest constructs and recursively build up complexity, mirroring how the compiler itself processes code.

posts.summary: This technical deep-dive explains how the Zig compiler's Sema (semantic analysis) stage transforms ZIR (Zig Intermediate Representation) into AIR (Analyzed Intermediate Representation). The post details how AIR is a fully-typed representation where comptime evaluation occurs, enabling generic types to be realized and compile-time optimizations to be performed. Through detailed code examples, Hashimoto walks through the anatomy of AIR instructions, the Value/Type/TypedValue system, and traces the step-by-step analysis of a simple addition function. The article demonstrates how comptime evaluation can pre-compute results and how the compiler handles both comptime-known and runtime values differently.
key_points: AIR is generated per-function (not per-file like ZIR), and only for exported or referenced functions via lazy analysis
The Sema stage performs all comptime evaluation, which is necessary because fully typing a Zig program requires resolving generic types at compile time
Values in Zig's compiler are untyped until paired with a Type to form a TypedValue - and types themselves can be values with type 'type'
The compiler emulates target platform properties during comptime evaluation, making cross-compilation safe and predictable
Sema maintains a ZIR-to-AIR mapping (inst_map) that allows AIR instructions to reference resolved ZIR instructions
money_quotes: AIR, which stands for 'Analyzed Intermediate Representation' is a fully typed intermediate representation, whereas ZIR is an untyped intermediate representation.

One of the reasons ZIR is untyped is because fully typing a Zig program requires comptime evaluation so that generic types (amongst other things) can be fully realized. Therefore, Sema also performs all the comptime evaluation of Zig programs. This is where the magic happens!
A type can also be a value in Zig. For example, the statement `const c = u8` is completely valid Zig: you're assigning the type `u8` to the constant `c` which itself has the type `type`.
The source for Sema is in `src/Sema.zig`. It is a very large file (over 18,000 lines at the time of writing this) and is self-described as 'the heart of the Zig compiler.'
For comptime-evaluated code, the Zig compiler emulates the properties of the target platform when necessary. This is a critical feature that makes comptime-evaluation safe and possible within Zig.
This implements Zig's 'lazy analysis.' This means that certain errors will not produce compiler errors unless the declaration is referenced.

You can see that the comptime evaluation was done and our addition was done in this stage so that the result is already pre-known. When this eventually gets translated down to machine code, the actual addition result is already pre-computed.
posts.key_insight: Zig's semantic analysis stage is where comptime magic happens - transforming untyped intermediate representation into fully-typed AIR while evaluating compile-time expressions and emulating target platform properties.

posts.summary: This post provides a deep dive into how Zig's built-in build system works internally. The key insight is that `zig build` actually compiles twice: first it compiles the `build.zig` file into a standalone build binary, then that binary orchestrates the actual project compilation by spawning subprocesses to the Zig compiler. The post walks through the entire pipeline, from how the build runner is located to how steps are defined declaratively and executed in dependency order. Mitchell demonstrates how to manually build and invoke the build binary to demystify the process, emphasizing that understanding these internals makes developers more productive.
key_points: The `zig build` command works in two phases: first compiling build.zig into a build binary, then executing that binary to orchestrate the actual build
The build binary doesn't embed a Zig compiler—it subprocesses back out to `zig build-exe` or similar commands, meaning you could theoretically point it at different Zig versions
Build steps are defined declaratively in the build function but executed later, which is a common source of confusion when developers try to read files that haven't been generated yet
The build system uses Zig's comptime capabilities to introspect function signatures, allowing flexible return types for the build function
Steps are executed in dependency order using a simple recursive approach with loop detection via flags rather than building a formal graph structure
money_quotes: Build systems are an extremely important detail of any software project. When they work, they can feel like magic: you execute a command, and after a series of potentially complicated steps, a working binary (or other artifact) is produced!
Understanding the internals of the build system are a way to remove that 'magic' and make it easier to get things done.
My primary takeaway from studying the internals of the Zig build system is that it's just Zig.
You can do anything you want in the build.zig file because it is compiled into a fully fledged executable for your current system.
I deeply believe that understanding the layer beneath the tools we use everyday makes us better tool users.
It eliminates any mystery by exposing the machines inner workings, and I tend to find the inner workings are always simpler than I expected.
Because the steps are declaratively defined, you must be thoughtful to understand when and where logic actually occurs.
posts.key_insight: The Zig build system's apparent magic dissolves when you understand it's just compiling your build.zig into an executable—and knowing this internal structure makes you a more effective user of the tool.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto presents a systematic five-step approach to understanding and contributing to complex software projects. The methodology begins with becoming a genuine user who builds something real, then learning to build the project from source before attempting to read code. The core learning technique is 'trace down, learn up'—following a feature's code path from outside to inside, then understanding components from innermost to outermost. The process concludes with studying recent commits as practice problems and making an initial small contribution to learn the human side of the project. Throughout, Hashimoto emphasizes joining the community, experimenting by breaking things, and avoiding the trap of trying to understand everything at once.
key_points: Build something real with the project before diving into internals—theoretical knowledge from documentation differs significantly from practical experience
Learn to build the project and run tests before reading code; you can't experiment effectively without a working build
Use 'trace down, learn up': trace a feature's code path without understanding details, then learn components starting from the innermost, least abstracted layers
Study recent commits as 'problem sets'—try to fix bugs yourself before looking at the actual solution to test understanding
First contributions take disproportionately long (hours for a three-line change), but this investment enables rapid future contributions
money_quotes: Don't read the code before learning to build the project.

A common mistake I see people make is getting lost for weeks or months and ultimately getting discouraged because they attempt to just read an entire project line by line. Stay focused and learn feature by feature.
I choose the innermost point to start because it is usually the most fundamental and least abstracted.
There is no supplement for getting your hands dirty and playing with the actual source code when trying to 'become a maintainer.'
If resources to learn the internals do not exist, try writing them yourself!
I think too many engineers look at stereotypically complex projects such as programming languages, browsers, databases, etc. as magic or as destined for higher-beings.
All projects were started by other humans. If they could do it, I can do it too. And so can you.
posts.key_insight: Complex projects become approachable through a disciplined progression: use it, build it, trace features without understanding, learn from the inside out, practice on real commits, then contribute small changes to learn the human process.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto recounts his naive approach to business banking as a 22-year-old startup founder, from opening a Chase account with $20,000 to managing $35 million four years later. He repeatedly ignored his banker Alex's calls, not understanding the significance of their relationship, then abruptly transferred all funds to Silicon Valley Bank without warning. Years later he discovered he had become a cautionary tale at Chase branches across Los Angeles, learned that Alex had faced consequences, and had to navigate a surreal process to close the forgotten account and withdraw $1M via cashier's check. The story culminates in his recognition that banks aren't fungible and relationships matter, even if no lasting harm came from his choices.
key_points: Banks are not interchangeable institutions - local branches and individual bankers have their own performance metrics, relationships, and stakes in your account
A startup account growing from $20K to $35M made the original banker a rising star, and its sudden departure had real professional consequences
Institutional knowledge spreads: HashiCorp became a case study for Chase branches on how startup financial patterns differ from typical businesses
Neglected accounts create problems: the forgotten Chase account accumulated over $100K in fraud before anyone noticed
Sometimes reasonable decisions made in good faith with limited knowledge still lead to cringe-worthy situations in hindsight
money_quotes: I figured: you put money in, you take money out, they're all the same.
Some wheels have started turning in Southern California, and I just hadn't known it yet.
I switched banks, that's all there is to it. Thanks and goodbye. I never talk to Alex ever again.
The teller looks at the paper, then looks at me, then looks back at the paper, then asks 'Are you the HashiCorp guy?'
And then, overnight, the account went to $0. Without talking to anyone, without any prior warning, that account was gone.
I literally asked: 'like, green paper money cash?' He says yes. The balance in the account is somewhere around $1M.
He literally writes down a phone number on a piece of paper. This is all feeling so surreal.
I want to make it clear that Chase could've been an excellent banking partner. I never gave them the chance.
posts.key_insight: Business banking relationships are not transactional utilities but human relationships with real stakes, and treating them otherwise can have unintended consequences for everyone involved.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto analyzes the current AI/LLM moment through the lens of cloud computing's rise, arguing that AI shares the same hallmarks of a genuine platform shift. Like early AWS, AI provides immediate practical value while remaining impractical for many enterprise contexts—a gap that will close over time. He predicts AI will force an evolution in software properties similar to 'cloud-native,' where products that adapt will outcompete those that don't. Crucially, AI has a migration story: it makes existing software better rather than requiring wholesale replacement, which historically enables industry-wide adoption.
key_points: AI shares cloud's key success indicator: immediate, undeniable value to early adopters, unlike crypto which never passed the 'okay, now what?' test
Current AI limitations (reliability, integration, tooling) mirror early cloud's impracticality, but these are tractable engineering problems, not fundamental impossibilities
Platform shifts force evolution in software properties—AI will likely make natural language interfaces a baseline expectation for all software
Technologies that bring along 'old friends' through migration paths scale to industry-wide impact; AI improves existing software rather than requiring replacement
If this is truly a platform shift, we're in extremely early innings with years of early-mover opportunity ahead
money_quotes: Every 10 or so years, a platform shift begins.
Immediate value is a really good sign and builds an early tribe of enthusiastic users that make a lot of noise. At the same time, immediate value is often mistaken for durable value.
All I know is someone sent me a bitcoin in 2010 and I thought 'okay, now what?' And I personally never really got past that stage even to now, 13 years later.
The impractical parts of AI aren't that it isn't useful, its that its hard to integrate or trust the usefulness of it at scale. This is a tractable problem, not some fundamental impossibility.
New technologies that require throwing away old technologies are harder to scale to industry-wide impact than new technologies that somehow bring along old technologies.
Recent AI developments make existing software better. Yes, they enable some revolutionary new takes for certain categories of software too, but they continue to make 'legacy' software better.
At the very least, I would caution against fully ignoring this one.
posts.key_insight: AI's staying power comes not from revolutionary replacement but from evolutionary improvement—it makes existing software better while opening new possibilities, the same pattern that enabled cloud computing's industry-wide adoption.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto argues that prompt engineering is a legitimate engineering discipline, distinct from what he calls 'blind prompting'—the crude trial-and-error approach most people use. He demonstrates a systematic methodology: define the problem, create a demonstration set with expected inputs/outputs, generate multiple prompt candidates, test them for accuracy across different approaches (zero-shot, few-shot), and choose based on cost-accuracy tradeoffs. The key insight is treating prompts like any other engineering artifact—with testing, measurement, and continuous improvement based on real-world failures.
key_points: Blind prompting (trial-and-error without testing) is fundamentally different from prompt engineering, which applies systematic experimental methodologies
Start simple: extract one piece of information, use literal text extraction rather than complex transformations, and let traditional programming handle deterministic tasks like date parsing
A demonstration set serves three purposes: measuring accuracy, specifying expected input/output shapes, and providing exemplars for few-shot prompts
Prompt selection is a cost vs. accuracy analysis—sometimes a less accurate prompt with 50% fewer tokens is the right business decision
Verification and continuous improvement are essential: log failures, grow the demonstration set, and develop better prompts over time
money_quotes: A lot of people who claim to be doing prompt engineering today are actually just blind prompting.
Engineering starts with not using a method for the method's sake, but driven by the belief that it is the right method.
The simpler the output, the easier it will be to get higher accuracy.
Language models are not human, so we can't automatically expect equivalent performance.

Don't try to force the LLM to output exactly perfect formats to start. Performing too much 'output shaping' in the LLM early on makes it difficult to separate an LLM's ability to perform some core task from its ability to structure the output.
This isn't necessarily the most accurate prompt. This is a cost vs. accuracy analysis based on model used, tokens required, and accuracy presented.
Most importantly, you have the data to make an informed decision.
posts.key_insight: Prompt engineering becomes real engineering when you apply systematic testing, measurement, and cost-accuracy tradeoffs rather than relying on trial-and-error intuition.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto advocates for using Nix alongside traditional Dockerfiles to solve the persistent problem of inconsistent environments across development, CI, and production. The approach uses Nix as a single source of truth for building software, then leverages multi-stage Docker builds to create minimal final images. While Dockerfiles alone are simple, they create duplicated effort and brittle configurations when combined with separate local dev and CI setups. The key insight is that Nix's benefits compound when the same configuration powers all environments, eliminating 'works on my machine' issues entirely.
key_points: Traditional setups require maintaining three separate environment configurations (local dev, CI, production), which inevitably drift and break
Nix serves as a single source of truth that works consistently across Linux, Mac, CI, and Docker with zero duplication
The Dockerfile approach uses multi-stage builds: Nix builds in a builder container, then only the closure of dependencies gets copied to a scratch-based final image
The Nix store closure represents the mathematically smallest possible set of files needed to run your application
Learning Nix has upfront costs but eliminates entire categories of environment-related problems permanently
money_quotes: I'm serious, I haven't had a 'works on my machine [but not on others]' issue in years. Not a single one.
It's like someone looking at a river and lamenting about having to wade through to cross it while I'm riding a bicycle across a bridge.
I'm actively trying to work through my Nix God complex.
In isolation, I submit to you that using Nix with Docker is mostly just harder than using Docker by itself.
These problems all go away with Nix. Nix is your single source of truth of what you need to build and run your software.
I think the real pain point is you want your environments to Just Work.
It does not depend on or conflict with your local system at all. If you have other versions of Python, Flask, etc. installed, it won't matter at all; your app is perfectly packaged.
posts.key_insight: The compounding benefits of using Nix across all environments—not just Docker—transform it from being harder than plain Dockerfiles to being dramatically easier while eliminating entire categories of 'works on my machine' problems.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto distinguishes between two modes of using language models: interactive prompting (conversational, human-driven) and transactional prompting (function-like, software-driven at scale). He argues that prompt engineering primarily benefits transactional use cases where objective correctness can be measured. The post adds another dimension—objectivity vs. subjectivity—noting that prompt engineering works best for tasks with objectively correct outputs like classification or information extraction. For subjective creative tasks, arriving at good prompts is more creative writing than engineering. Hashimoto concludes that both modes exist without invalidating each other, and that agents, despite being automated interactive processes, are transactional in nature and benefit from prompt engineering.
key_points: Interactive prompting is conversational and human-driven; transactional prompting treats the LLM as a function with inputs and outputs at high volume
Prompt engineering aims for accuracy and low cost, goals that naturally align with transactional use cases
The objective/subjective dimension matters: prompt engineering works well for objectively correct tasks but less so for creative or subjective outputs
Code generation exists on a spectrum—smaller tasks can be objectively specified, but larger tasks become increasingly subjective
AI agents are technically interactive but primarily transactional in usage, making them good candidates for prompt engineering
money_quotes: Prompt engineering is primarily about transactional prompting, and a considerable amount of confusion or negativity arises when an individual tries to apply it to interactive prompting.
Transactional prompting treats the language model more like a function in a programming language: you give it some input and desire some output.
In the same way that you cannot 'engineer' art to be objectively good or bad, it is impossible to 'prompt engineer' an LLM on a subjective task to be objectively good or bad.
Prompt engineering for interactive prompts is more akin to someone being an effective 'Googler' whereas prompt engineering for transactional prompts is closer to someone being a data scientist.
Prompt engineering attempts to turn an LLM into an objective, predictable, transactional API like any other API a program may interface with.
The larger the code generation task, the more subjective it becomes. Conversely, the smaller the code generation task, the more likely that test cases or the input language can be exactly specified.
posts.key_insight: Prompt engineering is most valuable when treating language models as transactional APIs for objective tasks, not as conversational partners for creative or subjective work.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto explains how to build truly native cross-platform GUIs by writing business logic in a systems language like Zig and platform-specific UI code separately. His terminal emulator achieves 93% shared Zig code with only 4% macOS-specific Swift code. The post walks through the technical pipeline: exporting C APIs from Zig, building static libraries, creating universal binaries with libtool and lipo, packaging as xcframeworks, and integrating with XCode. While the process involves many steps, each relies on decades-old, stable tooling that's unlikely to break. He also explains why Objective-C isn't viable—Apple's future is clearly Swift and SwiftUI.
key_points: Cross-platform apps can achieve native GUIs by separating business logic (Zig/Rust/C) from platform-specific UI code (SwiftUI/GTK)
Zig's 'export' keyword makes exposing C APIs trivial, enabling standard linking with any platform's native toolchain
The build pipeline requires libtool for bundling dependencies, lipo for universal binaries, and xcframework packaging for XCode integration
Each step uses decades-old, stable tools—nothing cutting edge or brittle
Objective-C is not viable for modern macOS development; Apple clearly wants developers using Swift and SwiftUI
money_quotes: Nowadays, most people just *don't* and fallback to using a non-native experience such as Electron instead.
As of the current date writing this post, 93% of my repository is business logic in Zig and C, and 4% is macOS-specific GUI code in Swift.
As a result, my terminal emulator is truly native: you get native Mac windows, Mac GUI components (buttons, text fields), etc. It looks and feels great.
All of the steps are tried and true -- usually decades-old -- operations and tools for working with system libraries. They're unlikely to be brittle going forward.
The payoff is worth it: you can get a truly native GUI experience while keeping almost all of your application logic cross-platform.
It is painfully clear that the future of Apple device programming is Swift.
You'd be really fighting what Apple *wants* you to do.
posts.key_insight: Building truly native cross-platform applications is achievable by leveraging C ABI compatibility and decades-old system tooling, allowing 90%+ code sharing while delivering platform-native experiences.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto shares his methodology for completing large technical projects, centered on maintaining motivation through continuous visible progress. Rather than planning everything upfront or building to perfection, he advocates decomposing projects into small pieces that yield tangible results quickly. Using his terminal emulator as an example, he demonstrates how prioritizing demos over finished components, embracing 'good enough' implementations, and becoming your own first user creates a sustainable feedback loop. The approach acknowledges that perfectionism often derails projects, and that seeing working code—even imperfect code—is what keeps engineers engaged over months of development.
key_points: Break large projects into sub-components where you can see results as soon as possible, not into comprehensive task lists
Automated tests provide early visible progress when building non-graphical components—watching test counts grow creates excitement
Sprint to demos rather than finished components; do not let perfection or known future improvements stop forward progress
Senior engineers often get bogged down building the perfect implementation only to discover the product itself sucks
Adopt your own software as quickly as possible and build only what you need—personal investment in the problem drives motivation
money_quotes: We've all experienced that feeling of excitement starting a new project. The first few weeks you can't wait to get on the computer to work. Then slowly over time you get distracted or make up excuses and work on it less.
As a broad generalization I've not found an engineer who doesn't get excited by a good demo. And the goal is to always give yourself a good demo.
Do not let perfection be an enemy of progress. Going further, do not let future improvements you know you'll have to make stop you from moving on to the next thing.

I've seen senior engineers get bogged down building the perfect thing and by the time they get a demo, they realize it sucks. The implementation doesn't suck, but the product or feature itself actually sucks.
Seeing the progression of '1 test passed', '4 tests passed,' '13 tests passed' and so on is super exciting to me. I'm running some code I wrote and it's working.
I always feel a lot of pride using software with code that I wrote and that usually helps keep me motivated to continue working on it.
I don't think you need to ship a project for it to be successful.
posts.key_insight: Sustained motivation on large projects comes from optimizing for continuous visible progress through demos and working tests, not from comprehensive planning or building perfect components.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto introduces the first devlog for Ghostty, a GPU-accelerated terminal emulator he's been building since 2021 using Zig. The post covers the technical stack (Zig, OpenGL/Metal, SwiftUI/GTK) and dives into two recent features: automatic shell integration injection that enables smoother prompt redrawing on resize, and auto-italicization of fonts that lack italic faces. Shell integration allows terminals and shells to communicate for better UX, though most developers don't have it configured. The font feature programmatically skews regular glyphs to create fake italics when the font doesn't provide them natively.
key_points: Ghostty is written in Zig with platform-specific frontends (SwiftUI on macOS, GTK on Linux) and GPU acceleration via OpenGL/Metal
Shell integration enables powerful features like proper prompt redrawing on resize, working directory inheritance, and active process detection—but most developers don't know about or use it
Ghostty now automatically injects shell integration configuration for fish and zsh, removing the need for manual setup
When fonts lack italic faces, Ghostty programmatically creates fake italics by applying a 15-degree skew transformation matrix during rasterization
Font fallback was fixed to try the regular style first before searching other fonts, preventing ugly mixed monospace/proportional rendering
money_quotes: Talking to at least my close circle of developer friends, most people do not know this and do not have this enabled or do not use a terminal emulator that supports it.
It won't look as good because it wasn't artisanally designed while sipping a single-origin pour-over but it'll... pass.

There are some complexities about drawing this glyph in a way that matches your configured font but this isn't about that -- just know those problems exist and figuring out the solution personally feels like some form of self-harm.
shell-specific (zsh code for zsh, fish code for fish, garbage for bash, etc.) configuration
Since Ghostty isn't public at the time of writing this, the terminal with the best shell integration today is Kitty.
posts.key_insight: Modern terminal emulators can dramatically improve user experience through shell integration and smart font handling, but these capabilities remain unknown to most developers.

posts.summary: This second Ghostty devlog covers two major technical improvements: implementing non-native fullscreen on macOS and fixing a subtle font blurriness bug on Linux. The non-native fullscreen feature required a substantial rewrite, moving from SwiftUI to AppKit for window and app lifecycle management while keeping SwiftUI for views. The Linux font blurriness was caused by a subtle floating-point rounding error in window padding calculations that led to fractional pixel values, which the GPU would round up and then downscale, introducing anti-aliasing blur. The post also announces that Ghostty will be free and open source, likely under GPLv3.
key_points: Non-native fullscreen on macOS required replacing SwiftUI app/window lifecycle with AppKit, turning a seemingly simple feature into an 800+ line change
A months-old Linux font blurriness bug was traced to floating-point rounding errors in padding calculations that caused off-by-one pixel mismatches between CPU and GPU rendering
The proper fix involved changing size data structures from floats to integers throughout, only converting to floats at the GPU boundary
Ghostty will be released as free and open source software, with the community leaning toward GPLv3
SwiftUI limitations forced architectural changes, but maintaining native macOS experience remains a priority despite the extra work
money_quotes: If you've been following Ghostty, I very proudly talk about how Ghostty is written in Zig but uses SwiftUI for macOS GUI.
Thorsten was able to delete a file named CursedMenuManager.swift and that's a good hint into the broader reason this was a good change.
I respect that there's a lot of people out there who really like Swift, but I'd much prefer to be working in Zig.
Sit around the campfire friends, because this is a very important software engineering lesson on the insidious nature of float math and float/int conversions.
If it was off by even one pixel, you'd get blurriness. Oh. My. God.
Downscaling introduces anti-aliasing, which by definition blurs edges. Hence, blurry fonts.
So this is also a lesson in using the right data type.
posts.key_insight: Floating-point math in graphics programming can cause subtle rendering bugs that are nearly impossible to reproduce, making integer types the safer choice for screen dimensions and padding values.

posts.summary: This devlog covers major progress on Ghostty's keyboard input handling, which required solving the complex problem of supporting non-US keyboard layouts, dead key sequences, and unicode character input across platforms. Mitchell implemented support for multiple keyboard protocols (xterm modified keys, fixterms, and Kitty Keyboard Protocol), making Ghostty one of only a handful of terminals to support all of them. The Linux/GTK experience received significant improvements including single instance mode, primary clipboard support, and automatic shell integration. The post emphasizes how expanding the beta tester pool, particularly to non-US users and Linux users, exposed critical issues that led to these improvements.
key_points: Keyboard input handling is surprisingly complex due to physical scancodes, logical key translation, dead key sequences, modifier consumption, and unicode character production - all of which must work cross-platform

Ghostty now implements xterm modified keys, fixterms, and Kitty Keyboard Protocol - making it one of only a few terminals (alongside iTerm, Foot, Kitty, and possibly WezTerm) to support all major keyboard protocols
Linux/GTK improvements include single instance mode for fast subsequent launches, primary (selection) clipboard support, and automatic shell integration via FHS directory layout detection
Physical key mapping is now optionally available via a 'physical:' prefix in keybindings, allowing users to bind based on physical key position rather than logical character output
Beta tester diversity (non-US keyboard users, Linux users) proved essential for exposing edge cases and driving quality improvements
money_quotes: I'm just a dumb US citizen typing on a dumb US-standard keyboard layout. On a US-standard keyboard, you type `a` and you get "a", you type `'` (apostrophe) and you get "'". You sit on a throne of lies.

When a person types a key on their keyboard, a few things happen. The keyboard sends a physical scancode. Software keyboard layout handling translates this scancode to an alternate scancode. The combination of modifiers and other keys may or may not then produce one or more characters.
I plan on writing an entire blog post dedicated to the eldritch horror that is keyboard layouts, input handling, terminal keyboard 'protocols', and OS-level APIs.
Ghostty not only has to handle these use cases (because it turns out non US-standard keyboard users exist) but Ghostty must also do this cross-platform.
Most terminals (Terminal.app, Windows Terminal, Alacritty, Warp) implement none of the keyboard protocols.
I really want Ghostty to be a top-tier GTK-based Linux terminal.
posts.key_insight: Building a truly cross-platform terminal emulator requires solving the surprisingly complex problem of keyboard input handling, where the seemingly simple act of pressing a key involves layers of translation between physical scancodes, logical keys, and character output that vary dramatically across keyboard layouts and operating systems.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto introduces Ghostty, a new terminal emulator written from scratch in Zig that aims to be fast, feature-rich, and native all at once—a combination he argues existing terminals fail to achieve. The talk covers the project's architecture, including its cross-platform core with native GUI implementations (Swift/AppKit for macOS, GTK for Linux), and explores several Zig patterns that make this possible. Hashimoto positions Ghostty not just as another terminal, but as a potential modern platform for text application development, analogous to how browsers serve GUI apps. The project was in closed beta at the time of the talk, with plans for a 1.0 FOSS release in 2024.
key_points: Existing terminals force a tradeoff between fast, feature-rich, and native—Ghostty aims to achieve all three simultaneously
Zig's comptime interfaces enable platform-specific implementations with zero runtime overhead, allowing truly native experiences on each OS
The architecture uses comptime data tables and type generation to process configuration at compile-time, reducing binary size and eliminating runtime costs
Ghostty integrates with Swift for macOS by exporting a C API that Swift can call, enabling modern Apple frameworks while maintaining the Zig core
The vision extends beyond being 'just another terminal' to making terminals a modern platform for text application development with features like progress bars, drag-and-drop, and better security
money_quotes: You have fast terminals, feature rich terminals, and native terminals. You can pick at most two properties to have.
Fast, features, native feel is not mutually exclusive.
I want the terminal to be a modern platform for text application development, analogous to the browser being a modern platform for GUI application development.
Browsers ship dozens (hundreds?) of new features per year. They innovate fast and excite application developers. This may be a bit much, but I think terminals can innovate faster than they are.
Speculate all you want, I don't care for this question and I don't care to answer it. I chose Zig, I like Zig, let's move on.
The @Type builtin lets you create types at comptime. It is ridiculously powerful but also ridiculously scary.
Many projects often have shell, Python, or other scripts to do data generation. With Zig, you can do it all in comptime.
The first released version will be version 1.0 or at worst a 1.0 release candidate. The goal of this heavy testing program is that we can launch out of the gate with a stable, feature rich project.
posts.key_insight: By leveraging Zig's comptime capabilities for zero-cost abstractions and platform-specific implementations, you can build software that achieves properties previously thought to be mutually exclusive.

posts.summary: This devlog covers Ghostty's growth from 30 to 100 beta testers, with waves now adding 15-20 people as stability improves. The post details significant GUI improvements on both macOS and Linux, including native Wayland support. A major technical challenge emerged around terminal identification: Ghostty's attempt to use `TERM=ghostty` instead of `TERM=xterm-ghostty` exposed bugs in Vim and other programs that incorrectly hardcode terminal names rather than querying terminfo. The post also covers implementing XTGETTCAP (a protocol for querying terminal capabilities via escape sequences) using Zig's comptime for compile-time optimization, and adding variable font support—a rare feature among terminal emulators.
key_points: Ghostty's closed beta has grown to 100 users, with each wave now adding 15-20 people as the terminal becomes more stable
Native GUI improvements on macOS and Linux emphasize platform-idiomatic design, with full Wayland support now available
Attempting to use `TERM=ghostty` instead of `TERM=xterm-ghostty` revealed bugs in Vim and other programs that hardcode terminal names instead of properly querying terminfo
XTGETTCAP implementation uses Zig comptime to generate a compile-time optimized lookup table, making terminfo queries extremely fast with zero allocations
Variable font support allows fine-tuning of font weight and width axes, a feature rare among terminal emulators
money_quotes: A lot of naughty programs do string matching on TERM to determine if a terminal supports some set of functionality. This is wrong and very naughty, do not do this!
Terminfo is an extremely interesting (primarily because it is so cursed) topic on its own.
One of Ghostty's stated goals is to have native experiences that make it feel like Ghostty was purpose-built for the platform you're on.
XTGETTCAP performance doesn't really matter at all, but our XTGETTCAP implementation is stupid fast because it is a compile-time optimized lookup table.
I'm proud that Ghostty community have been not only reporting issues, but also providing fixes.
These types of features really let you fine tune your terminal to be exactly how you want it and make it feel just right.
I hope to write a dedicated blog post on terminfo one day.
posts.key_insight: Building a proper terminal emulator means not just implementing features correctly, but also discovering and fixing bugs in the broader ecosystem where programs have accumulated bad assumptions over decades.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto argues that GitHub pull requests are fundamentally flawed because they treat branches as single mutable changesets, leading to chaos in code review. The current model causes reviews to become outdated mid-submission, loses context when contributors push changes during review, and makes it impossible to compare different states of a PR effectively. He proposes implementing versioned changesets where each PR version is immutable and feedback is attached to specific versions. This pattern, already proven in tools like Gerrit and Phabricator, would make reviews cleaner, safer, and more scalable for large projects.
key_points: GitHub PRs currently function as a single mutable changeset tracking a branch, causing reviews to become outdated or partially invalidated when contributors push changes
Reviewers and contributors working simultaneously creates race conditions where feedback loses context or references now-hidden code
Changesets would make each PR version immutable with a monotonic version number, preserving the exact state feedback was given against
Contributors could defer publishing new changesets until they're ready, avoiding the visibility of work-in-progress commits
Reviewers could approve specific changesets, even choosing non-latest versions if they prefer an earlier approach
money_quotes: The lifecycle of a GitHub pull request today is effectively one giant mutable changeset. This is a mess!
A reviewer can leave a review for a previous state the PR was in and it can become immediately outdated because while the review was happening the contributor pushed changes.
A line comment may say 'same feedback as the previous comment' but the previous comment is now gone/hidden because the contributor pushed a change that moved those lines.
Work-in-progress commits towards addressing review feedback become visible as soon as the branch is pushed.

In the world of changesets, feedback is attached to a changeset. If a reviewer begins reviewing a changeset and a new changeset is published, that's okay because the review as an atomic unit is attached to the prior changeset.

The contributor can also post multiple changesets with differing approaches to a problem in a single PR and the maintainer can potentially choose a non-latest changeset as the set of changes they want to merge.
posts.key_insight: GitHub's fundamental flaw is treating pull requests as mutable entities rather than versioned changesets, causing review feedback to constantly lose its context and making large-scale collaboration unnecessarily painful.

posts.summary: Terminal emulators struggle with modern Unicode characters like complex emoji because they traditionally process individual bytes or codepoints rather than grapheme clusters. The farmer emoji '🧑‍🌾' is actually three Unicode codepoints joined together, causing terminals to report widths of 2, 4, 5, or 6 cells depending on implementation. Grapheme clustering—the process of recognizing multiple codepoints as a single user-perceived character—solves this problem but breaks compatibility with existing terminal programs that assume wcwidth behavior. Mode 2027 is a proposed standard that allows programs to opt into grapheme clustering support, enabling terminals and programs to agree on character widths. The author implemented mode 2027 in Ghostty after initially being disappointed that proper grapheme clustering broke fish shell's prompt rendering.
key_points: Traditional terminals use wcwidth to determine character widths, which fails for multi-codepoint graphemes like emoji with Zero-Width Joiners
Grapheme clustering is stateful and requires tracking previous codepoints, making it non-trivial to add to existing programs
Mode 2027 is a proposal allowing programs to opt into grapheme clustering support, solving the compatibility problem
This isn't just about emoji—proper grapheme clustering is essential for correctly handling languages like Arabic that use multiple codepoints per character
Terminal multiplexers like tmux compound the problem since they and the underlying terminal may disagree on character widths
money_quotes: Copy and paste '🧑‍🌾' in your terminal emulator. How many cells forward did your cursor move? Depending on your terminal emulator, it may have moved 2, 4, 5, or 6 cells.
It turns out that a single 32-bit value is not adequate to represent every user-perceived character in the world.
Even though it produces the wrong values for multi-codepoint graphemes, at least the wrong values are often consistently wrong across terminal emulators.
My shell assumed my terminal used wcwidth, and my terminal assumed programs didn't care. We both were wrong, so I disabled grapheme clustering...
The terminals reporting '5' and '6' are living a weird reality.
If tmux and the terminal don't match, the cursors become out of sync and the resulting bugs can be comical.
Implementing a terminal emulator was certainly eye-opening for me to see the complexities in text processing and the burden of legacy behaviors.
posts.key_insight: Terminal emulators and programs must coordinate on character width calculations through standards like mode 2027, because the legacy wcwidth approach fundamentally cannot handle modern Unicode's multi-codepoint graphemes.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto provides an update on Ghostty's development, highlighting major new features including a terminal inspector (similar to browser dev tools), improved Asian language input support, custom shader capabilities, and an xterm compatibility audit. The beta testing group has grown from 100 to over 350 people, with stability improving dramatically—beta waves that once caused dozens of bug reports now handle 50+ new testers smoothly. The post emphasizes Ghostty's commitment to being a proper platform for terminal applications while maintaining high performance and compatibility standards.
key_points: The terminal inspector brings web-inspector-style debugging to terminal development, showing live keyboard input, terminal modes, cell metadata, and more—aimed at making terminal app development easier across all terminals
Asian language input (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) now works well after inviting testers from diverse timezones revealed nearly a dozen issues with IME handling and character rendering
Custom shaders allow CRT effects and accessibility customizations while using minimal resources (~1% CPU, ~2% GPU), leveraging Shadertoy's interface for easy shader development
Ghostty now follows xterm as the gold standard for terminal behavior, providing consistent answers to 'why does this feature behave this way?' questions
Nearly 1 in 6 beta testers contributes code, with almost 50 contributors and over 100 bug fixes in two months
money_quotes: A terminal is an application platform. I think the word 'platform' is thrown around way too much by people trying to believe their work is more important than it actually is.
A terminal is not interesting without the applications that run in it. Going further, a terminal is pointless without applications that run in it.
One way of increasing the quantity and quality of terminal applications is to make it easier to build for the terminal.
The general inefficiency of most modern software has taken us all for fools! CPUs and GPUs are super fast and this is barely doing anything.
It's been so long since this hardware has been readily available or practically used that most modern terminal emulators just kind of mimic each others behavior.
The primary practical use case for custom shaders is accessibility.
I'm not looking for investors. I hope no one interprets this paragraph in that way, but I feel I have to say this.
posts.key_insight: Building a great terminal emulator requires treating it as a genuine application platform, which means investing in developer tooling, internationalization, and rigorous compatibility testing rather than just adding flashy features.

posts.summary: This devlog details a series of performance optimizations that dramatically improved Ghostty's IO throughput. The improvements include SIMD-based parsing that processes plain text 7-16x faster, precomputed lookup tables for Unicode codepoint width calculation that eliminated 30% of character processing time, and optimized grapheme break detection that runs nearly 8x faster while using less than 1KB of memory. A community contributor also implemented CSI sequence fast-path parsing for control-sequence-heavy applications. The combined optimizations result in Ghostty being faster at reading plain text with full grapheme clustering than most terminals that don't perform grapheme detection at all.
key_points: SIMD instructions allow processing 16-32 bytes simultaneously instead of one byte at a time, achieving near-memcpy speeds for parsing
Custom precomputed lookup tables for codepoint width are faster than even the simpler but incorrect libc wcwidth API
Terminal-specific preconditions (no control characters, ASCII filtering) enable grapheme break detection via a sub-1KB lookup table
Real-world improvements ranged from 2-5x faster plain text throughput depending on UTF-8 complexity
Community contributions, particularly CSI fast-path parsing, improved control-sequence-heavy workloads by 1.4-2x
money_quotes: SIMD is a rare scenario where hand-writing assembly (or near it) often results in significantly better performance over a state of the art optimizing compiler.
Our SIMD pipeline is reading and parsing text at roughly the same speed as simply reading the data into memory.
Through profiling efforts, we determined that 30% of the time taken for every printed character was spent calculating codepoint width.
We now get correct results faster than the simple broken libc API.

I realized if you add some additional preconditions a terminal can guarantee, you can eliminate enough conditionals such that computing a lookup table of every possible codepoint pair plus state takes up less than 1KB of memory.
In almost every case Ghostty is faster at reading plain text while doing grapheme break detection over terminals that read plain text while doing nothing.
Every CPU cycle counts.
posts.key_insight: By exploiting domain-specific constraints unique to terminal emulators—like guaranteed absence of control characters in certain parsing stages—you can achieve correctness and speed simultaneously, often outperforming simpler but less correct implementations.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces his role as advisor to Polar, a platform helping developers get paid for their passion projects. He reflects on his own journey from 2010-2012 when he built Vagrant while working full-time, eventually leading him to found HashiCorp with venture capital. After leaving HashiCorp in 2023, he's focused on helping 'developers in the small' find sustainable paths beyond the VC route. He emphasizes that he only advises projects he actively uses, and is already using Polar for his terminal project Ghostty.
key_points: The VC-backed company path worked for HashiCorp but 'needn't be (and isn't) the only path' for developers wanting to work on their passions
His post-HashiCorp personal goal is helping developers have 'more choice and viability to work on their passion'
He only gets financially involved with projects he uses firsthand, preferring to be an active, present advisor rather than a passive investor
He's using Polar for Ghostty and actively working with 800+ beta members on project sustainability and contributor rewards
money_quotes: I was left at an impasse: cut down on the time I spent on my personal passion projects or figure out a way for my personal passion projects to support my future.
This path worked for me... but it needn't be (and isn't) the only path.
One of my personal goals is to help developers in the small.
I want developers to have more choice and viability to work on their passion.
I want users to have more confidence and transparency in the sustainability of the software they use (free or paid).
Those who know me well know that I only get financially involved with projects that I use myself.
I'm not interested in writing checks or accepting shares and sitting back.
posts.key_insight: The venture capital path to working on passion projects is valid but shouldn't be the only option—developers need more sustainable paths to get paid for their work.

posts.summary: This post explains how Zig's comptime feature enables conditional code compilation using standard Zig syntax rather than preprocessor directives or build system complexity. Hashimoto contrasts this with C's preprocessor macros and Go's build tags, both of which require separate languages or external tooling. The key advantage is that comptime conditions can be mixed with runtime conditions in the same expression, allowing code to reference libraries or types that may not exist in certain build configurations without causing compiler errors. He demonstrates this with a real example from Ghostty that checks both build-time and runtime library versions. The post also covers important gotchas around function boundaries and the need for explicit comptime keywords or inlining.
key_points: Comptime runs standard Zig code at compile time without a separate preprocessor language, making conditional compilation more natural and powerful
Unlike C preprocessors or Go build tags, Zig's comptime doesn't require external build systems to generate definitions or manage complexity
Comptime and runtime conditions can be mixed in the same expression, with the compiler smart enough to skip unreachable code paths entirely
Code referencing unavailable libraries or types won't cause compiler errors if protected by a comptime condition that evaluates to false
Comptime doesn't automatically cross function boundaries—you need explicit comptime keywords or inline functions to maintain the optimization
money_quotes: I admit that when I first started looking into Zig, I thought this was a gimmick. 'How much could I possibly want to actually do at comptime, anyways?' I thought. Two years into using Zig for a non-trivial project, comptime is used all over and is by far my favorite feature about Zig.
The first thing to notice is the code is standard Zig. There is no preprocessor language or special syntax.

Importantly, this means I can use the library in the runtime conditions as well as the code block and I won't get compiler errors if the library isn't available or isn't the right version or if I reference types that only exist in newer versions of the library.

To drive the point home, let's consider how we might have done this in C. In C, we would have probably used a build system such as CMake that creates a one-time program and attempts to compile it to determine if it should define a preprocessor flag or not. Ugh.
It's a feature that I didn't know I needed until I had it, and one that I miss when I'm working in other languages.
I don't need a complex build system or external tools to manage my platform-specific code. I can just write Zig code and the compiler takes care of the rest.
posts.key_insight: Zig's comptime eliminates the need for preprocessor languages and complex build systems by allowing conditional compilation to be expressed in standard Zig code that can seamlessly mix compile-time and runtime conditions.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto demonstrates how to use Zig's comptime features to create type-safe subsets of tagged unions, solving a real problem in Ghostty where keybind actions need to be handled separately by terminal and application code. The naive solutions—using catch-all else clauses or explicitly listing ignored cases—either sacrifice compiler safety or result in verbose, poorly self-documenting code. By leveraging comptime type generation with @typeInfo and @Type builtins, he creates a ScopedAction function that generates union types containing only actions belonging to a specific scope. The solution provides compile-time exhaustiveness checking, self-documenting function signatures, and zero runtime cost for the type generation itself.
key_points: Tagged unions with catch-all else clauses lose compiler safety when new cases are added, but exhaustively listing ignored cases is verbose and doesn't express intent in the type signature
Zig's comptime allows generating new types at compile time by introspecting existing types with @typeInfo and reifying new ones with @Type
The scope() function that classifies actions doesn't need to know whether it runs at comptime or runtime—comptime executes the full language
The inline else pattern in switch statements generates code for each unhandled case while providing the tag as a compile-time known value
This approach provides compiler-enforced exhaustiveness, self-documenting function signatures, and centralizes the scope classification in one maintainable location
money_quotes: The problem is that the Action union contains both terminal-specific actions and application-wide actions.
When there is a catch-all case, the compiler can't error if I add a new action to the Action union and forget to handle it.
Since Zig has comptime code execution, and comptime code execution is the full language, we can use functions that take parameters at comptime and return things like... types.
