Note that our scope() function above has no idea if it is being executed in comptime or runtime. It doesn't care. It's just a normal function. Comptime is fucking cool that way.
All of this logic happens at compile time. There is no runtime cost to doing any of this.

I'm a type pragmatist. I believe leaning on the type system to enforce correctness is a powerful tool. At the same time, I believe some languages take this too far and make the type system a burden. Zig strikes a nice balance for me.
posts.key_insight: Zig's comptime enables generating specialized subset types from tagged unions at compile time, providing both type safety and self-documenting APIs without runtime overhead.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces a $300,000 personal donation to the Zig Software Foundation, paid in two annual installments. He traces his journey with Zig from following the project in 2019 to becoming an active contributor and building Ghostty in the language. The donation reflects his family's philosophy of supporting independent software projects that embody building for the love of building. He emphasizes Zig's strong management, transparent funding model, and clear path to broader adoption as reasons for the investment.
key_points: The donation is structured as $150,000 annual installments over two years, with the first already transferred
Hashimoto's Zig involvement evolved from observer (2019) to contributor (2022) to building Ghostty entirely in Zig (2023)
The family specifically targets independent projects that can be transformative while encouraging a culture of building for the love of building
About a third of ZSF funding comes from individual donations, making personal contributions significant to the project's sustainability
Hashimoto broke his usual privacy around philanthropy because he believes his public support could meaningfully help the project
money_quotes: We want to support independent software projects that we believe can be transformative and impactful, both as a way to give back to the community that gave me so much but more importantly as a way to highlight and encourage a culture of building for the love of building.
Zig was started as and continues to be a huge passion project.
It is well managed, the community is strong, and the funding model is transparent and sustainable.
As a technology, it is ambitious and innovative, but still practical and pragmatic.
It has a long way to go to reach stability and broader industry adoption, but I believe it has a clear path and opportunity to get there.
Our philanthropy is generally private, but I'm making an exception since I think my public support of Zig has a chance to really help the project due to my background.
posts.key_insight: Supporting independent, passion-driven software projects through substantial personal investment can both give back to the community and encourage a culture of building for the love of building.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces that Ghostty, his terminal emulator project developed over nearly two years with 2,000 private beta testers, will be publicly released as open-source under MIT license in December 2024. The project aims to be the best drop-in replacement for existing terminal emulators by combining speed, rich features, and platform-native GUIs—a combination Hashimoto felt was unnecessarily absent in the market. The extensive private beta allowed him to prioritize family time after his first child while ensuring stability. Beyond 1.0, he envisions libghostty as a cross-platform library enabling a diverse ecosystem of terminal applications, and expanding terminal functionality to make text-based applications more attractive to build and use.
key_points: Ghostty eliminates the false choice between speed, features, and native GUI that existing terminal emulators force users to make
The first public release will be version 1.0 (no ZeroVer) due to nearly two years of private beta testing with 2,000 users
The project uses truly native GUI toolkits (AppKit on macOS, GTK on Linux) rather than just using them to create windows
libghostty, the core cross-platform library, will eventually be released standalone to enable an ecosystem of terminal applications
Hashimoto views this as 'technical philanthropy'—giving back while scratching his own itch, with no plans for commercialization
money_quotes: I felt that the existing terminal emulators pushed an unnecessary choice between speed, features, and platform-native GUIs.

The most common feedback I've received from people before they've used Ghostty is skepticism that any of this makes a difference. The most common feedback I've received from people after they've used Ghostty is that they didn't realize how much of a difference it makes and they can't imagine going back.
I am nostalgic for the days when software was released when it was ready.
I like to imagine that if stores like CompUSA still existed, Ghostty 1.0 would be boxed, shrink-wrapped, and on the shelf ready for purchase (but also free and open-source).
I'm surprised how much criticism I received for this approach and the number of entitled people who seemed to believe that they were owed something just because I liked sharing my work and progress.
I view my work on Ghostty as a form of 'technical philanthropy;' a means for me to give back while also scratching my own itch and having fun.
I believe that text-based applications have a valuable niche in the software ecosystem and I want to help modernize the platform.
Most importantly, I hope people feel how much love and care I've put into Ghostty and that it brings joy to their daily work.
posts.key_insight: A terminal emulator can be fast, feature-rich, and platform-native simultaneously—users just didn't know they were settling for less until they experienced all three together.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto reflects on the release of Ghostty 1.0, a terminal emulator he started in 2022 as a way to learn Zig and graphics programming with no intention of releasing it publicly. What began as a learning project evolved into a serious endeavor when he recognized opportunities for improvement in existing terminals. The controversial private beta, while generating unintended hype and frustration, successfully allowed him to manage his bandwidth during his first year of fatherhood while cultivating an exceptional community of contributors. He remains committed to Ghostty as a passion project, with plans to release a standalone libghostty library in 2025 that could have broader impact on the terminal ecosystem.
key_points: Ghostty started as a personal learning project for Zig and graphics programming, not intended for public release
The private beta was primarily designed to manage personal bandwidth during first-time parenthood, not to generate hype
The Zig and platform-native GUI architecture proved highly successful, with over 90% shared code across platforms
The beta community of 5,000 users within a 28,000-member Discord was instrumental in making Ghostty polished and feature-rich
Future plans include stabilizing core features and releasing libghostty as a standalone library for other projects to build upon
money_quotes: I've always been a fan of the terminal, my entire career was built around shipping terminal-first software. But they're a solved problem, right? That's what I thought.
It's not perfect, it's not done, it's not for everyone. But it's mine. It's a reflection of my values and my vision for what a terminal could be.
You can't ship a terminal emulator that emulates only half the features Vim needs. You can't incrementally improve a terminal emulator below a certain threshold of functionality.
I didn't think many people at all would be interested in a terminal emulator. I thought I was building boring software for a niche audience. No hype!
Zig has been an absolute joy to work with. I have fun writing Zig every single day and that fun hasn't diminished a bit over two years.
The Ghostty community is a group of people who are passionate about terminals but also about performance, design, software quality, doing things right and for the right reasons.
I believe nothing in this world is accomplished alone. I may have started Ghostty alone but I didn't finish it alone.
posts.key_insight: What starts as a personal learning project can evolve into something meaningful when you recognize that 'solved problems' often have unexplored tradeoffs worth pursuing.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces eight new subsystem maintainers for Ghostty, the terminal emulator project that went public about a month ago but had a two-year private beta. Rather than appointing general core maintainers, Ghostty follows a subsystem model where contributors become authorities over specific areas like terminal emulation, GTK, fonts, rendering, and shell integration. All new maintainers emerged organically from the private beta community, having contributed dozens of PRs and actively helped others. The role grants autonomy to define roadmaps and merge PRs but explicitly carries no obligation to review anything—it's about trust and authority, not work requirements.
key_points: Ghostty uses a subsystem maintainer model rather than general core maintainers, matching expertise to specific project areas
All eight new maintainers were active contributors during the two-year private beta, demonstrating both technical skill and community helpfulness
Subsystems emerge organically based on where contributors show knowledge and excitement, not from top-down project planning
Maintainership explicitly carries no time obligations—it's a role of trust and authority, not a job with review requirements
The goal is scaling the project by giving trusted people autonomy to make decisions and ship improvements faster
money_quotes: Adding additional, independent maintainers is an important part of any open source project's long-term health and stability.
Rather than promoting 'core maintainers' who are responsible for every part of the project, we've identified key subsystems within the project with subject matter experts.
The exact 'subsystems' emerge organically as contributors show themselves to be knowledgeable, excited, and interested in a particular area of the project.
Issues can be resolved more quickly and improvements can be shipped faster when trusted maintainers have autonomy to make decisions within their subsystems.
Notably, maintainers have NO OBLIGATION to review pull requests or issues in their subsystem.

This isn't a job! It is a role of trust and authority and the expectation is that maintainers will use their best judgement and work on what they find interesting and rewarding for themselves and the project.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable open source governance comes from matching authority to organic expertise and explicitly removing obligation from volunteer maintainership.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto clarifies his personal definition of 'as code' in 'X as Code' terminology, explaining he means 'a system of principles or rules' rather than 'as programming.' As the creator of Terraform, he's often misunderstood on this point. His intent was always about inscribing knowledge—getting it out of people's heads into a shareable, versionable system. While codified knowledge often becomes computer-readable with automation benefits, he argues this doesn't mean all modern programming requirements should apply to 'as code' systems.
key_points: The 'code' in 'X as Code' refers to 'a system of principles or rules' (like a code of conduct), not programming code
The primary goal is inscribing knowledge so it can be shared, versioned, and iterated upon
Computer-readability and automation are secondary benefits, not the core purpose
Modern programming requirements shouldn't automatically apply to 'as code' systems
Hashimoto didn't coin 'Infrastructure as Code'—it predates Terraform and his career
money_quotes: My intent with 'X as Code' was always to get knowledge out of people's heads and into a more inscribed system.
Once inscribed, knowledge and process can be shared, versioned, iterated upon, etc.
I don't believe that implies that all the requirements of modern programming should be applied to 'as code' systems.
The definitions of things is a social process, after all. Where the herd goes, so will we all.
Don't create new market categories, enter existing ones and let your competitors bear the cost of educating the market.
posts.key_insight: The 'code' in 'Infrastructure as Code' was meant to evoke a codified system of rules for inscribing knowledge, not a mandate to apply all the complexity of modern software engineering to configuration.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto describes the complete rewrite of Ghostty's GTK application to fully embrace the GObject type system from Zig, verified with Valgrind throughout. The previous approach of avoiding GObject led to a mess of lifetime management bugs where either Zig or GTK memory would be freed but not both. By embracing GObject's reference counting, features like configuration reloading became conceptually simpler, and the team could adopt modern GTK technologies like Blueprint. The Valgrind verification process revealed that while dozens of memory issues existed at C API boundaries, the Zig codebase itself had only one leak and one undefined memory access—a surprisingly positive result that validates Zig's memory safety features.
key_points: Avoiding the GObject type system led to an entire class of bugs around mismatched lifetimes between Zig and GTK memory
Embracing GObject's reference counting simplified complex tasks like configuration reloading by letting property change notifications ripple through the application naturally
Valgrind testing revealed only one memory leak and one undefined access in the Zig codebase, but dozens of issues at C API boundaries
Whatever language you use to interface with C APIs, safety is only as good as understanding the API semantics and writing good wrappers
This is Mitchell's 5th time rewriting the Ghostty GUI from scratch, each iteration teaching valuable lessons carried forward
money_quotes: Whatever your feelings are about OOP and memory management, the reality is that if you choose GTK, you're forced into interfacing in some way with the GObject type system. You can't avoid it.
Well you can avoid it and we did avoid it. And it leads to a mess trying to tie the lifetimes of your non-reference-counted objects to the reference counted ones.
There was an entire class of bug that kept popping up in the Ghostty GTK application that could basically be summed up as: the Zig memory or the GTK memory has been freed, but not both.
Our Zig codebase had one leak and one undefined memory access. That was really surprising to me (in a good way).
All other memory issues revolved around C API boundaries.
Whatever language you're using to interact with it, the safety you're guaranteed is only as good as understanding the semantics of the API and writing a good wrapper.
There are a lot of academic or theoretical discussions about what Zig does or does not do and whether that's good or bad. Those are valuable discussions to be had, but so are empirical results.
posts.key_insight: Fighting against a framework's native paradigms creates more problems than it solves—embracing the GObject type system eliminated an entire class of memory bugs and simplified the codebase, while Valgrind verification showed Zig's safety features work well until you hit C API boundaries.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto argues that technical non-profits are significantly worse at fundraising than other charitable organizations, despite having eager potential donors. He identifies key failures: unclear donation methods, poor articulation of value and financial needs, lack of impact reports, and insufficient human connection with donors. The post emphasizes that non-profits are fundamentally sales-driven organizations and must treat donor relations with the same rigor as business development. Hashimoto draws on his experience as both a donor and entrepreneur to provide a concrete roadmap for improvement.
key_points: Tech non-profits fail at basic donation logistics—many lack clear instructions for DAFs, stock transfers, employer matching, and other common giving vehicles beyond credit cards
Technical people assume their project's value is self-evident and fail to answer fundamental questions about why money is needed, how it's spent, and what impact it creates
Impact reports are critical but most tech non-profits either skip them entirely or produce amateur versions that read like product keynotes rather than accountability documents
Human connection is 'unreasonably effective'—phone calls, video meetings, and in-person dinners with donors are essential sales activities that tech organizations avoid
Non-profits are sales-driven organizations despite their mission focus, and successful ones have people aggressively working on the 'business' of donations
money_quotes: It may sound obvious, but you want to make it as easy as possible to initiate the financial transaction. And yet, a lot of non-profits make this needlessly difficult.

I, too, used to have the attitude of 'the value is obvious' or perhaps the idea that 'if you build it, they will come.' This is true for a small, small segment of the 'market,' but for the much larger segment, you have to educate.

Non-profit organizations are sales-driven organizations. You have to engage in a lot of the standard tactics a business has to in order to attract and retain donors in order to pursue your mission statement.
A donor generally doesn't want to be part of a small group of donors. I specifically care deeply that the organization will have a healthy group of donors so that I'm just a piece.
How one deals with failure often speaks more loudly than how one deals with success.

A for-profit is a machine that takes money and returns more money to investors. A non-profit is a machine that takes money and returns social impact to donors. They're very different, and yet uncomfortably alike.
Honestly, become friends. I genuinely mean this.
posts.key_insight: Technical non-profits fail at fundraising because they treat donations as a passive byproduct of good work rather than an active sales function requiring dedicated effort, clear communication, and genuine human relationships.

posts.summary: Hashimoto argues that checking boxes and meeting specifications is insufficient for creating great work. Every interaction with software evokes a feeling in users, and that feeling is as much a part of the requirements as any functional specification. The difference between adequate and exceptional work lies in whether creators actually use and live with what they build. You cannot rely solely on metrics and demos—you must experience your own work to understand how it truly feels.
key_points: Metrics, specifications, and demos fail to capture the emotional experience of using software
The desired feeling users should have is an implicit requirement that's often overlooked
Great features feel like they were always meant to be there—they make you smile and want to use them again
Creators must sit with, use, and live with their work to understand its true quality
money_quotes: But you didn't feel it. You didn't feel it.
The feeling matters. The feeling is part of the work. The desired feeling is part of the requirements.
When you feel it, you know. The feature makes you smile when you use it.
It fits right in, like it was always meant to be there.
This is the difference. This is what metrics, specifications, and demos miss. They don't capture the feeling.
You can't stop at checking the boxes on paper. You have to sit with it, use it, live with it.
You have to feel it.
posts.key_insight: The emotional experience of using software is an essential requirement that cannot be measured by specifications or demos—creators must live with their work to truly understand its quality.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces libghostty, an embeddable library for terminal emulation that he first envisioned over two years ago. The first component, libghostty-vt, will be a zero-dependency library for parsing terminal sequences and maintaining terminal state, extracted from Ghostty's battle-tested core. The post argues that terminal emulation is a deceptively complex problem that hundreds of applications implement poorly with ad-hoc solutions. By providing a correct, fast, and portable C API, libghostty aims to eliminate this duplicated effort across the industry. The Zig API is available now for testing, with a C API coming soon and a stable release targeted within six months.
key_points: Terminal emulation appears simple but is riddled with unexpected complexities—even major products like JetBrains editors and Apple Terminal.app have significant bugs
libghostty-vt will be zero-dependency (not even libc), making it widely portable to macOS, Linux, Windows, embedded devices, and WASM
The library inherits Ghostty's real-world benefits: SIMD-optimized parsing, excellent Unicode support, fuzzing, and compatibility with protocols like Kitty Graphics
Future libghostty components will include input handling, GPU rendering, and complete GTK widgets and Swift frameworks
The project is seeking early adopters and feedback to help design the API before stabilization
money_quotes: Terminal emulation is a classic problem that appears simple on the surface but is riddled with unexpected complexities and edge cases.
Beyond correctness, implementing any form of terminal emulation is a waste of time for most developers.
Terminal emulation is not the core business of JetBrains, Visual Studio Code, GitHub, Vercel, Render, etc.
I've spent the better part of 3 years working on terminal emulation and we're still finding weird edge cases.
libghostty is the next frontier for Ghostty and I think it has the ability to make a far larger impact than Ghostty can as a standalone application itself.
The 'alpha' quality is with respect to the API itself. The core logic is shared with Ghostty and is extremely stable and proven in the real world.
posts.key_insight: By extracting Ghostty's battle-tested terminal emulation into a zero-dependency embeddable library, libghostty can have far greater industry impact than the standalone application by eliminating redundant, buggy implementations across hundreds of products.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto shares real-world build time improvements after upgrading Ghostty to Zig 0.15.1, demonstrating significant speedups across all build scenarios. The build script compilation dropped from 7 seconds to under 2 seconds, while full uncached builds improved from 41 to 32 seconds. Most impressively, libghostty-vt incremental builds now complete in under a second using Zig's self-hosted backend. These improvements come despite Ghostty still relying on LLVM for most builds and incremental compilation not yet being functional, suggesting even greater gains are coming.
key_points: Build script compilation improved 4x faster (7sec to 1.7sec), directly impacting first-time builds from source
Full Ghostty builds dropped from 41 to 32 seconds even while still using LLVM backend
libghostty-vt achieves sub-second incremental builds using Zig's self-hosted x86_64 backend
Current improvements are just the beginning—once LLVM is fully replaced and incremental compilation works, builds should reach millisecond-level speeds
The Zig team's multi-year investment in custom code generation, linkers, and compiler improvements is paying off
money_quotes: Andrew Kelley famously (or infamously, depending on your views) said 'the compiler is too damn slow, that's why we have bugs.'
A sub-second build time for a non-trivial library is amazing.
Previously, I might tab out to read an email between builds or tests, but now its so fast I can stay in flow in my terminal.
I've loved betting on Zig for Ghostty, and I love that they're focusing on compile times.
These improvements are real, and they're here now. And I suspect in the next couple years, the results posted today will look downright slow.
posts.key_insight: Zig 0.15.1 delivers substantial build time improvements that are already transforming daily development workflows, even before the most impactful optimizations like incremental compilation are fully realized.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto details how he shipped a non-trivial Ghostty feature—unobtrusive macOS update notifications—using AI-assisted coding across 16 sessions costing $15.98. He emphasizes that effective AI use requires domain expertise, careful planning, and treating AI as an assistant rather than replacement. The post walks through his complete process: prototyping UI, hitting walls where AI failed, extensive cleanup sessions, backend development, and simulation testing. He stresses that he always reviews and often rewrites AI-generated code, and that the real value isn't just speed but the ability to make progress while doing other things.
key_points: AI works best for prototyping and inspiration—Hashimoto often prompts agents, throws away everything, and redoes it manually, finding the 'zero to one' stage where AI excels
Breaking work into small, focused chunks is essential; never send an agent to build a full feature at once, and always review a plan before implementation
When AI enters the 'slop zone' repeatedly failing to fix bugs, it's time to step back, educate yourself, and think critically—AI becomes a liability at that point
Clean, well-documented code with good view model structure sets up both humans and AI agents for success in future sessions
The 'final manual review' is super important—never ship AI-written code without thorough review, and never ship code you don't understand
money_quotes: I believe good AI drivers are experts in their domains and utilize AI as an assistant, not a replacement.
AI is no longer the solution; it is a liability.
I very often use AI for inspiration. I will very often prompt an agent, throw away everything it did, and redo it myself.
I find the 'zero to one' stage of creation very difficult and time consuming and AI is excellent at being my muse.
If the agent figures it out and I don't understand it, I back it out. I'm not shipping code I don't understand.
Adding documentation is a really important step because it helps reaffirm your own understanding of the code as well as educate future agents.
I sometimes tongue-in-cheek refer to this as the 'anti-slop session'.
Please don't ever ship AI-written code without a thorough manual review.
posts.key_insight: Effective AI-assisted coding requires domain expertise to guide the AI, knowing when to take back control, and never shipping code you don't fully understand.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces that Ghostty, his open-source terminal emulator, is now fiscally sponsored by Hack Club, a 501(c)(3) non-profit. This structure ensures Ghostty remains free and open source, prevents any possibility of a 'rug pull' or commercial acquisition, and enables tax-deductible donations. Hashimoto emphasizes that foundational infrastructure like terminals should be stewarded by mission-driven, non-commercial entities. The arrangement includes full financial transparency, contributor compensation, and legal protections. His family is also personally donating $150,000 directly to Hack Club to support their broader mission.
key_points: Fiscal sponsorship through Hack Club provides legal protections against mission changes, fund diversion, or commercial sale of Ghostty
All financial transactions will be publicly transparent through Hack Club Bank's ledger system
No funds will benefit Hashimoto personally despite being both the largest donor and project lead
The structure enables contributor compensation, upstream dependency support, and community event funding
Hashimoto's family is donating $150,000 directly to Hack Club beyond the 7% administrative fees
money_quotes: I believe infrastructure of this kind should be stewarded by a mission-driven, non-commercial entity that prioritizes public benefit over private profit.

A non-profit structure provides enforceable assurances: the mission cannot be quietly changed, funds cannot be diverted to private benefit, and the project cannot be sold off or repurposed for commercial gain.
I want to squelch any possible concerns about a 'rug pull'.
Despite being decades-old technology, terminals and terminal-related technologies remain foundational to modern computing and software infrastructure.
Instead of donor fees going to a for-profit management company or covering pure overhead of a single project, the fees go to another non-profit doing important work in the tech community.
I want to lay bricks for a sustainable future for Ghostty that doesn't depend on my personal involvement technically or financially.
posts.key_insight: Critical developer infrastructure like terminal emulators should be governed by non-profit structures that legally bind projects to public benefit, preventing commercial capture while enabling sustainable community funding.

posts.summary: Ghostty had a significant memory leak that caused one user to experience 37 GB of memory consumption after 10 days of uptime. The leak stemmed from a bug in the terminal's PageList memory management system, specifically in a scrollback optimization that reused pages. When non-standard (larger than normal) memory pages were reused during scrollback pruning, their metadata was reset to standard size while the underlying mmap allocation remained large, causing munmap to never be called when freeing them. The bug was particularly triggered by Claude Code's output patterns, which frequently produced multi-codepoint grapheme outputs requiring non-standard pages. The fix simply prevents reusing non-standard pages during scrollback pruning, properly destroying them and allocating fresh standard pages instead.
key_points: Ghostty uses a PageList data structure with a memory pool for standard-sized pages, while non-standard larger pages bypass the pool and require explicit munmap calls
A scrollback optimization that reuses the oldest page as the newest introduced a bug where non-standard page metadata was reset to standard size without resizing the actual memory allocation
The leak only manifested under specific conditions - Claude Code's multi-codepoint grapheme outputs created the perfect storm to trigger it at scale
VM tags on macOS Mach kernel proved instrumental in identifying and verifying the fix by allowing PageList allocations to be tracked separately

Despite comprehensive leak prevention measures including valgrind, Zig leak-detecting allocators, and macOS Instruments, this bug slipped through because tests didn't reproduce the specific triggering conditions
money_quotes: A few months ago, users started reporting that Ghostty was consuming absurd amounts of memory, with one user reporting 37 GB after 10 days of uptime.
The limited conditions that triggered the leak are what made it particularly tricky to diagnose.
Think of it like buying standard-sized shipping boxes: most things people ship fit in a standard box, and having a standard box comes with various efficiencies.

Eventually, we'd free the page under various circumstances. At that point, we'd see the page memory was within the standard size, assume it was part of the pool, and we would never call munmap on it. A classic leak.
The rise of Claude Code changed this. For some reason, Claude Code's CLI produces a lot of multi-codepoint grapheme outputs which force Ghostty to regularly use non-standard pages.
I want to be explicit that this bug is not Claude Code's fault. Claude Code is simply exercising Ghostty in a way that exposes this long-standing bug.
This has worked really well to date, but unfortunately it didn't catch this particular leak because it only triggers under very specific conditions that our tests didn't reproduce.
Remember that reproduction is the key to diagnosing and fixing memory leaks!
posts.key_insight: Memory management bugs often hide in optimization code paths that are only exercised under specific conditions, and even comprehensive testing strategies can miss them until real-world usage patterns evolve.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto introduces Tripwire, a Zig library he built for Ghostty that injects failures into code to test error handling paths, specifically errdefer cleanup logic. He explains that errdefer—Zig's mechanism for undoing partial effects when errors occur—is ironically one of the most error-prone parts of Zig programs because error paths are rarely exercised in testing. Tripwire lets developers place named failure points in code that trigger errors during tests but compile to nothing in release builds, using Zig's comptime features for true zero-cost abstraction. By combining Tripwire with Zig's testing allocator, developers can verify that errdefer cleanup actually works correctly by detecting memory leaks when errors are injected. Integrating Tripwire into just a handful of places in Ghostty immediately uncovered roughly six errdefer bugs. He encourages others to copy the single-file, MIT-licensed library into their own projects.
key_points: Error cleanup via errdefer is paradoxically one of the most error-prone parts of Zig programs because error paths are rarely exercised in tests and only cognitively reviewed during development

Tripwire works by placing named failure points before fallible operations that can be triggered in tests to force errdefer execution, while compiling to zero overhead in release builds via Zig's comptime and inline calling conventions
Iterating through all possible failure points with std.meta.tags provides complete error path coverage in a single test
Integrating Tripwire into only a handful of places in Ghostty immediately uncovered ~6 errdefer bugs that had never been known to trigger in production
Zig's testing allocator serves as the verification mechanism—if errdefer cleanup is missing or wrong, the test fails with a detected memory leak
money_quotes: Ironically, error cleanup is one of the most error-prone parts of Zig programs and is a consistent source of resource leaks and memory corruption.

Error codepaths are typically much less frequently executed and triggering them in tests can be difficult. As a result, they usually are only cognitively reviewed once or twice during development, and never truly exercised until a user hits them in production.
I ultimately grew tired of eyeballing error handling code and hoping it was correct, or spending hours trying to write tests that create a perfect-but-fragile scenario to trigger an exact error path.
Outside of tests, Tripwire emits no machine code and uses no memory; it is completely optimized away.
I integrated Tripwire into Ghostty in only a handful of places and immediately uncovered many bugs.
And most importantly, the bugs are now fixed paired with unit tests that verified they exist! If I remove the fix, the tests fail!
posts.key_insight: Error cleanup code is paradoxically the most error-prone part of programs because it's rarely tested, and fault injection libraries like Tripwire solve this by making error paths systematically exercisable at zero runtime cost.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto describes his gradual journey from AI skeptic to productive AI user, structured as a six-step progression. He began by abandoning chatbot interfaces in favor of agentic tools, then forced himself through a painful period of reproducing his manual work with agents to build expertise. He discovered efficiency gains by running agents during off-hours, delegating high-confidence tasks while working on other things manually, and engineering 'harnesses' (AGENTS.md files and verification tools) to prevent recurring agent mistakes. His current goal is to always have a background agent running on useful work, though he emphasizes measured adoption over hype and respects others' choices not to use AI at all.
key_points: Chatbot interfaces are fundamentally limited for coding; agents that can read files, execute programs, and make HTTP requests are the minimum viable AI tool for real productivity.
Forcing yourself to reproduce manual work with agents is painful but essential for building the expertise to know what agents are and aren't good at — including knowing when not to use them.
Running end-of-day agents for research, idea exploration, and issue triage provides a 'warm start' the next morning without requiring overnight loops.
Turning off agent notifications and controlling when you check on them is critical — context switching destroys the human's productivity more than the agent's output is worth.
'Harness engineering' — investing time to prevent agents from repeating mistakes via AGENTS.md and verification tooling — is the highest-leverage activity for improving agent output quality.
money_quotes: Instead of giving up, I forced myself to reproduce all my manual commits with agentic ones. I literally did the work twice.
Using an agent for something it'll likely fail at is obviously a big waste of time and having the knowledge to avoid that completely leads to time savings.
Instead of trying to do more in the time I have, try to do more in the time I don't have.

Very important at this stage: turn off agent desktop notifications. Context switching is very expensive. It was my job as a human to be in control of when I interrupt the agent, not the other way around.
Anytime you find an agent makes a mistake, you take the time to engineer a solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again.
I could now focus my coding and thinking on tasks I really loved while still adequately completing the tasks I didn't.
I don't want to run agents for the sake of running agents. I only want to run them when there is a task I think would be truly helpful to me.
I really don't care one way or the other if AI is here to stay, I'm a software craftsman that just wants to build stuff for the love of the game.
posts.key_insight: Productive AI adoption requires painful deliberate practice to learn what agents can and can't do, then systematically engineering harnesses and verification tools so agents produce correct results autonomously while you focus on the work you actually enjoy.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto argues that the most effective path to software adoption has shifted from building polished mainline applications to creating high-quality building blocks that others assemble. He illustrates this with Ghostty's growth: the app reached one million daily update checks in 18 months, while libghostty reached multiple millions of daily users in just two months. AI agents are accelerating this shift by excelling at gluing together proven components, lowering the barrier to entry that previously limited the ecosystem. The positives include lower quality bars for derivative works, outsourced R&D, reduced maintenance burden, and greater awareness in niche communities. He acknowledges that closed-source commercial software is at a disadvantage as AI agents prefer open and free alternatives. Rather than fighting or fully submitting to this shift, Hashimoto advocates for pragmatically embracing the building block economy.
key_points: AI is mediocre at building from scratch but excels at gluing together well-documented, proven components — making building blocks the optimal unit of software distribution
The building block model benefits maintainers by outsourcing R&D, reducing maintenance burden, and making it easier to say no to feature requests
Mainline applications aren't disappearing but are becoming more stable and purposeful, powered by a larger user base and ecosystem-driven feature discovery
Closed-source commercial software is at a structural disadvantage because AI agents consistently prefer open and free alternatives
The barrier to entry for assembling software from components has collapsed, transforming the quantity and diversity of what gets built
money_quotes: The most effective way to build software and get massive adoption is no longer high quality mainline apps but via building blocks that enable and encourage others to build quantity over quality.
AI is okay at building everything from scratch, but it is really good at gluing together high quality, well documented, and proven components.
The natural barrier to entry of understanding the component pieces well enough to even slap them together was high enough to limit the ecosystem. This barrier is now gone.
You can argue that 99% of the stuff coming out of these factories is total crap, but you can't argue the sheer quantity of stuff coming out.
It is so much easier now as a maintainer to look at what others are doing, see working proof of concepts, and decide what you want to bring back to mainline. There's way less talk and way more walk.
Agents will more readily pick open and free software over closed and commercial. At the time of writing this article, this is an objective truth.
We have to accept that building blocks and software factories rule everything around us and accept and internalize the consequences of that.
The point is the shift has already happened. We're living in it.
posts.key_insight: In the age of AI-driven software assembly, the highest-leverage strategy is to build high-quality open building blocks rather than polished applications, because agents and developers alike prefer proven components they can glue together.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto details his work modifying simdutf, a high-performance Unicode library, to be buildable without libc++ or libc++abi dependencies. This was the final C++ standard library dependency blocking libghostty-vt from being fully portable across embedded, WebAssembly, and freestanding environments. He walks through the technical approach: creating a stl_compat.h shim for standard library types, replacing function-local statics with translation-unit statics to avoid __cxa_guard_acquire, and providing weak symbol shims for __cxa_pure_virtual. He emphasizes preserving ABI compatibility except when the new flag is enabled, and added CI audits to prevent regression. The bulk of the post reflects on the contributor etiquette of submitting a 3,000-line PR, where he spent more time on validation and PR preparation than on the code itself.
key_points: Removing libc++ improves portability to embedded, WebAssembly, and freestanding environments while simplifying cross-compilation and static linking
libc++abi dependencies are sneakier than libc++ because the compiler silently emits ABI calls for ordinary language features like function-local statics
The fix involved a stl_compat.h compatibility shim, replacing function-local statics with translation-unit statics, and weak symbol shims for pure virtual slots
ABI compatibility is fully preserved when SIMDUTF_NO_LIBCXX is not set, making this a non-breaking feature addition for existing users
Submitting a large PR responsibly requires more effort on validation and communication than on the code itself, especially in the age of AI-assisted contributions
money_quotes: Getting something working and getting something merged are two different things.
The sneakier part is libc++abi, which provides the C++ ABI, including things like exception handling, virtual function tables, RTTI, etc.
They show up because the compiler quietly emits calls into the C++ ABI runtime for ordinary-looking language features.
Ironically, the full diff took me about 2 hours to put together, but the extra validation work and PR preparation took me about 3 hours.
I spent more time on the human boundary than the code itself, as we should out of respect for the effort maintainers put into their projects.
And I know the burden of recent AI slop.
Since by definition someone using SIMDUTF_NO_LIBCXX is not interested in libc++, these ABI breakages felt like a feature rather than a bug.
So I put in the effort I would want from an all-star contributor and tried to be that person for the simdutf maintainers.
posts.key_insight: Removing hidden C++ ABI dependencies requires both deep toolchain knowledge and, equally important, the human discipline to present large contributions in a way that respects maintainers' time.

posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces that Ghostty, his terminal emulator project, will be leaving GitHub due to persistent reliability issues. He reflects on his deep, 18-year personal connection to GitHub as user #1299, describing it as the place that has made him happiest throughout his life. Despite this emotional attachment, he explains that near-daily outages have made GitHub untenable for serious work, blocking PR reviews and CI pipelines for hours at a time. The move will be incremental, with a read-only mirror kept on GitHub, while his personal projects remain there for now.
key_points: Hashimoto has used GitHub daily for over 18 years and considers it deeply personal — he started Vagrant partly hoping it would get him hired there
He kept a journal tracking GitHub outages and found that almost every day for a month had a negative impact on his ability to work
The problem isn't Git itself but the infrastructure around it: issues, PRs, Actions, and CI/CD pipelines that block work during outages
The migration will be incremental, with a read-only mirror preserved at the current GitHub URL
He acknowledges being publicly mean and hurting feelings of GitHub employees, framing his anger as a product of how much he cares
money_quotes: Some might call this sick, but my hobby and work and passion all align and for most of my life they got to also live in one place on the internet: GitHub.
I love GitHub more than a person should love a thing, and I'm mad at it.
This is no longer a place for serious work if it just blocks you out for hours per day, every day.
I want to be there but it doesn't want me to be there. I want to get work done and it doesn't want me to get work done. I want to ship software and it doesn't want me to ship software.
After 18 years, I've got to go. I'd love to come back one day, but this will have to be predicated on real results and improvements, not words and promises.
Did you know I started Vagrant (my first successful open source project) in large part because I hoped it would get me a job at GitHub?
posts.key_insight: Even the most devoted users will eventually leave a platform when persistent reliability failures make it impossible to do serious work, no matter how deep the emotional attachment.
